narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, including your middle family income bracket and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 61.9% chance that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage outcomes for the school. The primary factor driving this prediction was your Year 1 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the lower end compared to the average of 5.71; this early academic performance strongly pushed the model toward forecasting bar failure, as it signals potential challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework. Next in influence, your LSAT score of 35—below the average of 36.70—pulled somewhat in the opposite direction, suggesting a moderate boost toward passing, but not enough to offset the top concern. Your middle family income also contributed toward the failure prediction, as the model associates it with slightly higher risk compared to higher brackets in the dataset. Overall, these elements made your profile riskier than typical admitted students, who generally show stronger early grades to balance out any test score dips. While your above-average undergrad GPA and full-time status were positive notes, they weren't decisive enough here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many succeed despite similar starts—but the model prioritizes patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push closer to or above the 36.70 average, as even a small gain there could shift your prediction favorably. Strengthening your recent academic decile, perhaps through targeted prep or additional coursework, would address the biggest red flag from Year 1. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews or bridge courses to demonstrate growth. With focused improvements, reapplying next cycle could yield a different outcome—your foundation is there, and persistence pays off for students like you.
